```bash
sudo systemctl enable nginx            # start at boot (creates symlink)
sudo systemctl disable nginx           # do NOT start at boot (removes symlink)
sudo systemctl enable --now nginx      # enable AND start immediately (most common)
```

**Key distinction:**
- `start`/`stop` = affects the **current state** only. Lost on reboot.
- `enable`/`disable` = affects **boot behavior** only. Does not change current state.
- `enable --now` = does both. **This is almost always what you want.**

### Checking Status

```bash
systemctl is-active nginx              # prints "active" or "inactive"
systemctl is-enabled nginx             # prints "enabled" or "disabled"
systemctl --failed                     # list all failed units
systemctl list-units --type=service    # list all loaded services
```

---

## Unit File Structure

Here is a typical service unit file:

```bash
cat /lib/systemd/system/nginx.service
```

```ini
[Unit]
Description=A high performance web server
After=network.target
Requires=network.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=www-data
ExecStart=/usr/sbin/nginx -g 'daemon off;'
ExecReload=/bin/kill -s HUP $MAINPID
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

### Section Breakdown

**[Unit]** — Metadata and dependencies:
- `Description` — human-readable name
- `After` — start this unit AFTER the listed units (ordering)
- `Requires` — this unit NEEDS the listed units (hard dependency — if they fail, this fails)
- `Wants` — soft dependency (if they fail, this still starts)

**[Service]** — How to run the service:
- `Type=simple` — the process started by ExecStart IS the service (most common)
- `Type=forking` — the process forks and the parent exits (traditional daemons)
- `User` — run as this user (not root)
- `ExecStart` — the command to start the service
- `ExecReload` — the command to reload configuration
- `Restart=on-failure` — restart if the process exits with a non-zero code
- `Restart=always` — restart no matter how it exits
- `RestartSec=5` — wait 5 seconds before restarting

**[Install]** — Boot integration:
- `WantedBy=multi-user.target` — enable this service when the system reaches multi-user mode (standard server boot)

### Creating a Custom Service

```bash
sudo vim /etc/systemd/system/myapp.service
```

```ini
[Unit]
Description=My Application
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=ubuntu
WorkingDirectory=/opt/myapp
ExecStart=/usr/bin/python3 /opt/myapp/app.py
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
Environment=PORT=8080

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

```bash
# CRITICAL: reload systemd after creating/modifying unit files
sudo systemctl daemon-reload

# Enable and start
sudo systemctl enable --now myapp

# Check status
systemctl status myapp
```

**Always run `daemon-reload`** after creating or modifying unit files. Without it, systemd uses the old cached version.

---

## journalctl — Querying Logs

Systemd captures all service output (stdout and stderr) in the **journal**. `journalctl` is the tool to query it.

```bash
# All logs (oldest first)
journalctl

# Logs for a specific service
journalctl -u nginx
journalctl -u nginx -u mysql           # multiple services

# Follow logs in real-time (like tail -f)
journalctl -u nginx -f

# Last 50 lines
journalctl -u nginx -n 50

# Logs since a time
journalctl --since "1 hour ago"
journalctl --since "2024-03-15 10:00:00"
journalctl --since "yesterday"

# Filter by priority (severity)
journalctl -p err                      # errors and above
journalctl -p warning                  # warnings and above

# Current boot only
journalctl -b

# Previous boot (useful after a crash)
journalctl -b -1

# Kernel messages only
journalctl -k
```

### Combining Filters

```bash
# Errors from nginx in the last hour
journalctl -u nginx -p err --since "1 hour ago"

# All errors from current boot
journalctl -b -p err
```

---

## Chapter Summary
### Overriding Unit Files with systemctl edit

```bash
sudo systemctl edit nginx
# Opens editor for /etc/systemd/system/nginx.service.d/override.conf
```

```ini
[Service]
Restart=always
RestartSec=10
LimitNOFILE=65536
```

```bash
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart nginx
```

This is the proper way to customise a service — never edit files in `/lib/systemd/system/`.

### Masking Services

```bash
sudo systemctl mask nginx      # completely prevent starting (even manually)
sudo systemctl unmask nginx    # reverse the mask
```

`mask` creates a symlink to `/dev/null`. Stronger than `disable` — use for security hardening.



- **PID 1** is the init daemon — systemd on modern systems.
- **SysVinit** was sequential and script-based; **systemd** is parallel with dependency management.
- **Runlevels** (0-6) map to **targets** (multi-user.target, graphical.target, rescue.target).
- **Unit file locations:** `/etc/systemd/system/` (local, highest priority) > `/run/` > `/lib/` (distro defaults).
- **systemctl:** `start`/`stop` = now, `enable`/`disable` = boot, `enable --now` = both.
- **Unit file sections:** [Unit] for dependencies, [Service] for execution, [Install] for boot.
- **daemon-reload** after any unit file change.
- **journalctl:** `-u` service, `-f` follow, `-n` lines, `--since`, `-p` priority, `-b` boot.

### Q: What is the difference between `systemctl start` and `systemctl enable`?

`systemctl start nginx` starts the service **right now** but has no effect on boot — if the server reboots, nginx will not start automatically. `systemctl enable nginx` configures the service to start **at boot** but does not start it now. `systemctl enable --now nginx` does both: starts immediately AND configures for boot. This is almost always what you want.

### Q: What is the difference between init (SysVinit) and systemd?

**SysVinit** used sequential shell scripts in `/etc/init.d/`, starting services one after another. It had no dependency management, no automatic restart, and slow boot times. **Systemd** starts services in parallel based on dependency graphs, provides automatic restart policies, unified management via `systemctl`, and structured logging via `journalctl`. Systemd uses unit files (declarative INI format) instead of shell scripts, making service configuration more consistent and less error-prone.

title: Filesystem Deep Dive
description: Understand inodes, hard links vs symlinks, mount points, /etc/fstab, disk usage tools (df, du), and the virtual /proc and /sys filesystems.
content:
## Beyond Files and Directories

In Chapter 2, you learned to navigate the filesystem. Now we go deeper — into how the filesystem actually works under the hood. Understanding inodes, links, and mounts is essential for troubleshooting disk issues and is a favourite interview topic.

---

## Inodes — The Real Identity of a File

When you think of a file, you think of its name. But to the filesystem, the name is just a label. The real identity of a file is its **inode** (index node).

An **inode** is a data structure stored on disk that contains all metadata about a file:

- File type (regular file, directory, symlink, etc.)
- **Permissions** (rwxr-xr-x)
- **Owner UID** and **Group GID**
- **File size** in bytes
- **Timestamps:** access time (atime), modification time (mtime), change time (ctime)
- **Hard link count** — how many directory entries point to this inode
- **Pointers to data blocks** — where the actual file content is stored on disk

### What Is NOT in the Inode?

**The filename is NOT stored in the inode.** This is a critical concept and a common interview question.

The filename is stored in the **directory entry**. A directory is simply a list of name → inode number mappings:

```
Directory: /home/ubuntu/
  .          → inode 131073
  ..         → inode 2
  notes.txt  → inode 131080
  script.sh  → inode 131081
```

Every directory contains at least two special entries:
- `.` (dot) — points to the directory's own inode
- `..` (dot-dot) — points to the parent directory's inode

### Viewing Inode Information

```bash
# Show inode numbers
ls -i /etc/passwd
# 262198 /etc/passwd

# Detailed inode info
stat /etc/passwd
#   File: /etc/passwd
#   Size: 2847        Blocks: 8          IO Block: 4096   regular file
# Device: 801h/2049d  Inode: 262198      Links: 1
# Access: (0644/-rw-r--r--)  Uid: (0/root)   Gid: (0/root)
# Access: 2024-03-15 10:30:00
# Modify: 2024-03-15 10:30:00
# Change: 2024-03-15 10:30:00
```

### Where Inodes Are Stored

The filesystem divides the disk into **block groups**. Each block group has an **inode table** (fixed-size array of inodes) and **data blocks** (where file content lives). The number of inodes is set when the filesystem is created and **cannot be changed** without reformatting.

### Inode Exhaustion

A disk can run out of inodes even if it has free space. This happens when you have millions of tiny files (common with mail servers or package caches).

**Symptoms:** Writes fail with "No space left on device" but `df -h` shows free space.

**Diagnosis:**

```bash
df -h              # shows disk space — looks fine
df -i              # shows inode usage — 100% used!
```

**Fix:** Delete unnecessary small files, or recreate the filesystem with more inodes.

---

## Hard Links vs Symbolic Links

### Hard Links

A **hard link** is another directory entry pointing to the **same inode**. The file's data exists only once on disk, but it has multiple names.

```bash
# Create a hard link
ln original.txt hardlink.txt

# Both point to the same inode
ls -i original.txt hardlink.txt
# 131080 original.txt
# 131080 hardlink.txt          ← same inode number!

# The link count is now 2
stat original.txt
# Links: 2
```

**Key properties of hard links:**
- Same inode, same data — changes to one are visible in the other
- **Survives deletion of the original** — deleting `original.txt` only removes one directory entry. The data persists as long as at least one link exists (link count > 0)
- **Cannot cross filesystem boundaries** — both names must be on the same filesystem
- **Cannot link to directories** — to prevent circular loops in the directory tree

### Symbolic (Soft) Links

A **symbolic link** (symlink) is a special file that contains a **path** to another file. It is like a shortcut.

```bash
# Create a symlink
ln -s /var/log/syslog ~/syslog-link

# The symlink has its own inode
ls -i ~/syslog-link
# 131090 syslog-link            ← different inode from the target

# ls -l shows the target
ls -l ~/syslog-link
# lrwxrwxrwx 1 ubuntu ubuntu 15 Mar 15 10:30 syslog-link -> /var/log/syslog
```

**Key properties of symlinks:**
- Has its **own inode** — it is a separate file containing a path string
- **Breaks if the target is deleted** — becomes a "dangling" symlink (points to nothing)
- **Can cross filesystem boundaries** — because it stores a path, not an inode reference
- **Can link to directories** — commonly used for this

### Comparison Table

| Feature | Hard Link | Symbolic Link |
|---|---|---|
| Points to | Same inode | A path (string) |
| Own inode? | No (shares inode) | Yes (separate inode) |
| Survives target deletion? | **Yes** | No (becomes dangling) |
| Cross filesystems? | **No** | Yes |
| Link to directories? | **No** | Yes |
| Created with | `ln target link` | `ln -s target link` |

---

## Mounting Filesystems

In Linux, to access a filesystem (a disk partition, USB drive, network share), you **mount** it onto a directory in the tree.

```bash
# Mount a partition
sudo mount /dev/sdb1 /mnt/data

# Mount a network share (NFS)
sudo mount -t nfs server:/share /mnt/nfs

# List all mounted filesystems
mount
findmnt                                # tree view (cleaner)
findmnt -t ext4                        # filter by filesystem type
```

### /etc/fstab — Persistent Mounts

Mounts created with `mount` are lost on reboot. To make them permanent, add an entry to `/etc/fstab`:

```bash
cat /etc/fstab
# device          mount-point  type  options        dump  fsck-order
/dev/sda1         /            ext4  defaults       0     1
/dev/sdb1         /mnt/data    ext4  defaults       0     2
UUID=abc-123      /mnt/backup  xfs   defaults,nofail 0    0
```

**Always test fstab changes before rebooting:**

```bash
sudo mount -a                          # mount everything in fstab
```

If there is an error in fstab, `mount -a` will show it. A bad fstab entry can prevent the system from booting.

---

## Disk Usage Tools

### df — Disk Free (Filesystem Level)

```bash
df -h                                  # human-readable sizes
# Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
# /dev/sda1        50G   32G   16G  67% /
# /dev/sdb1       100G   45G   50G  48% /mnt/data

df -i                                  # inode usage
# Filesystem      Inodes  IUsed   IFree IUse% Mounted on
# /dev/sda1      3276800 245000 3031800    8% /
```

### du — Disk Usage (Directory Level)

```bash
du -sh /var/log                        # total size of /var/log
# 2.3G    /var/log

du -sh /var/log/*                      # size of each item in /var/log
du -sh /var/log/* | sort -rh | head    # largest items first
```

### The Difference Between du and df

This is a common interview question. `du` and `df` can report different numbers for the same filesystem:

- **du** walks the directory tree and sums up file sizes. It only counts files it can see.
- **df** reads filesystem metadata (superblock). It counts ALL allocated space, including:
  - Filesystem metadata and journal
  - **Deleted files that are still open** — if a process has a file open and you delete it, the space is not freed until the process closes the file. `df` counts this space as used; `du` does not see the file.

**Troubleshooting:** If `df` shows a disk is full but `du` shows less usage, look for deleted-but-open files:

```bash
sudo lsof | grep deleted
```

---

## Virtual Filesystems: /proc and /sys

### /proc — Process and Kernel Information

`/proc` is a **virtual filesystem** — nothing is stored on disk. The kernel generates its contents dynamically when you read them.

```bash
# System information
cat /proc/cpuinfo                      # CPU details
cat /proc/meminfo                      # memory details
cat /proc/loadavg                      # load averages
cat /proc/version                      # kernel version
cat /proc/uptime                       # seconds since boot

# Per-process information (replace PID)
cat /proc/1234/status                  # process state, memory, UIDs
cat /proc/1234/cmdline                 # command that started the process
ls /proc/1234/fd/                      # open file descriptors
cat /proc/1234/oom_score               # OOM killer score
```

Tools like `ps`, `top`, `free`, and `lsof` all read from `/proc` under the hood. They are just user-friendly wrappers.

### /sys — Hardware and Kernel Interface

`/sys` is also virtual. It provides a structured interface to kernel data about hardware, drivers, and kernel subsystems.

```bash
ls /sys/class/net/                     # network interfaces
cat /sys/class/net/eth0/address        # MAC address
ls /sys/block/                         # block devices (disks)
cat /sys/block/sda/size                # disk size in sectors
```

---

## Chapter Summary
### Viewing Block Devices with lsblk

```bash
lsblk
# NAME   MAJ:MIN RM  SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINT
# sda      8:0    0   50G  0 disk
# ├─sda1   8:1    0   49G  0 part /
# └─sda2   8:2    0    1G  0 part [SWAP]
# sdb      8:16   0  100G  0 disk
# └─sdb1   8:17   0  100G  0 part /mnt/data
```

`lsblk` is your first command for understanding disk layout.

### Common Filesystem Types

- **ext4** — Default on Ubuntu/Debian. Supports shrinking and growing. Most widely used.
- **xfs** — Default on RHEL/Amazon Linux. Excellent for large files. Can grow but **cannot shrink**.
- **btrfs** — Copy-on-write with snapshots and checksums. Used by some modern distros.

Check type with `df -T` or `blkid`.

### fstab Mount Options

```
UUID=abc-123  /mnt/backup  xfs  defaults,nofail  0  2
```

Common options:
- `defaults` — rw, suid, dev, exec, auto, nouser, async
- `nofail` — don't fail boot if device is missing (critical for non-root mounts)
- `noexec` — prevent executing binaries (security hardening for /tmp)
- `nosuid` — ignore setuid bits
- `ro` — mount read-only

The 5th field (0) is for dump backups (rarely used). The 6th field is fsck order: 1 for root, 2 for others, 0 to skip.



- **Inodes** store all file metadata (permissions, owner, size, timestamps, data block pointers). The **filename is NOT in the inode** — it is in the directory entry.
- **Inode exhaustion:** disk has free space but writes fail → check with `df -i`.
- **Hard links:** same inode, survives deletion, can't cross filesystems, can't link directories.
- **Symlinks:** separate file containing a path, breaks if target deleted, can cross filesystems, can link directories.
- **mount** attaches filesystems; **/etc/fstab** makes mounts persistent. Test with `mount -a`.
- **df -h** shows filesystem-level space; **du -sh** shows directory-level usage. They can differ because df counts deleted-but-open files.
- **/proc** and **/sys** are virtual filesystems generated by the kernel.

### Q: What are inodes? What is NOT stored in an inode?

An **inode** is a data structure on disk that stores all metadata about a file: permissions, owner UID/GID, file size, timestamps (access, modify, change), hard link count, and pointers to the data blocks where the file's content is stored. Each file has a unique inode number. The **filename is NOT stored in the inode** — it is stored in the directory entry, which is a mapping of name → inode number. This is why hard links work: multiple names can point to the same inode.

### Q: What is the difference between hard links and soft (symbolic) links?

A **hard link** is another directory entry pointing to the **same inode** as the original file. Both names are equal — deleting the original does not affect the hard link because the data persists as long as any link exists. Hard links cannot cross filesystem boundaries and cannot link to directories. A **symbolic link** is a separate file (with its own inode) that contains the **path** to the target. If the target is deleted, the symlink becomes dangling (broken). Symlinks can cross filesystems and can link to directories.

### Q: What is the difference between du and df? Why might they show different numbers?

**du** walks the directory tree and sums file sizes — it only counts files it can see. **df** reads filesystem metadata and reports total allocated space. They differ because df includes: (1) filesystem metadata and journal overhead, and (2) space held by **deleted files that are still open** by a running process. If a process has a log file open and you delete it, `du` no longer sees it but `df` still counts the space as used. Find these with `lsof | grep deleted`.

title: Bash Scripting
description: Write robust shell scripts with variables, conditionals, loops, functions, error handling, debugging, and best practices.
content:
## Why Bash Scripting?

Every command you have typed so far has been a one-liner. But what if you need to run 20 commands in sequence, make decisions based on results, and repeat the process daily? That is what **shell scripts** are for.

A shell script is a text file containing a sequence of commands. Instead of typing them one by one, you run the script and it executes them all. Scripts are the foundation of automation in Linux.

---

## Your First Script

```bash
#!/bin/bash
echo "Hello, World!"
echo "Today is $(date)"
echo "You are logged in as: $(whoami)"
```

Save this as `hello.sh`, make it executable, and run it:

```bash
chmod u+x hello.sh
./hello.sh
```

### The Shebang Line

The first line `#!/bin/bash` is called the **shebang** (or hashbang). It tells the kernel which interpreter to use to run this script. Without it, the system may use a different shell (like `dash` on Ubuntu), which has different syntax.

**Always include `#!/bin/bash`** as the first line of your scripts.

---

## Defensive Settings: set -euo pipefail

Put these at the top of every script, right after the shebang:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -e          # Exit immediately if any command fails
set -u          # Treat unset variables as errors (catches typos)
set -o pipefail # Propagate failures through pipes
```

Or combined on one line:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
```

### Why Each Matters

**set -e** — Without this, a failing command is silently ignored and the script continues. With it, the script stops immediately on any error.

```bash
# Without set -e: rm fails silently, then the script DELETES THE WRONG THING
cd /nonexistent
rm -rf *              # This runs in your HOME directory!

# With set -e: the script stops at 'cd /nonexistent'
```

**set -u** — Without this, a typo in a variable name silently expands to an empty string.

```bash
# Without set -u:
rm -rf $TAGET_DIR/    # Typo! $TAGET_DIR is empty → becomes: rm -rf /

# With set -u: "TAGET_DIR: unbound variable" error, script stops
```

**set -o pipefail** — Without this, a pipeline's exit code is the exit code of the LAST command only.

```bash
# Without pipefail: grep fails but wc succeeds, so the pipeline "succeeds"
grep "pattern" nonexistent_file | wc -l

# With pipefail: the pipeline fails because grep failed
```

---

## Variables

```bash
# Assignment (NO spaces around =)
NAME="Alice"
COUNT=42
FILE="/var/log/syslog"

# Using variables (prefix with $)
echo "Hello, $NAME"
echo "File: $FILE has $(wc -l < $FILE) lines"

# Command substitution — capture command output
DATE=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)
HOSTNAME=$(hostname)
IP=$(hostname -I | awk '{print $1}')

# Arithmetic
COUNT=$((COUNT + 1))
TOTAL=$((10 * 5 + 3))

# Default values
NAME=${1:-"default_name"}              # use $1 if set, otherwise "default_name"
LOG_DIR=${LOG_DIR:-"/var/log"}         # use $LOG_DIR if set, otherwise "/var/log"
```

### Quoting Rules

```bash
# Double quotes: variables ARE expanded
echo "Hello, $NAME"                    # Hello, Alice

# Single quotes: NOTHING is expanded (literal)
echo 'Hello, $NAME'                    # Hello, $NAME

# Backticks (old style, avoid) vs $() (modern, preferred)
DATE=`date`                            # old style — hard to nest
DATE=$(date)                           # modern — can nest: $(echo $(date))
```

**Rule:** Always double-quote your variables: `"$VAR"` not `$VAR`. Unquoted variables break on spaces and glob characters.

```bash
# BROKEN — if FILE contains spaces, this fails
cat $FILE

# CORRECT
cat "$FILE"
```

---

## Conditionals

```bash
if [ "$VAR" = "value" ]; then
    echo "Match"
elif [ "$VAR" = "other" ]; then
    echo "Other match"
else
    echo "No match"
fi
```

### Test Operators

**String tests:**

```bash
[ "$VAR" = "value" ]       # string equals
[ "$VAR" != "value" ]      # string not equals
[ -z "$VAR" ]              # string is empty (zero length)
[ -n "$VAR" ]              # string is NOT empty
```

**Numeric tests:**

```bash
[ "$N" -eq 10 ]            # equal
[ "$N" -ne 10 ]            # not equal
[ "$N" -gt 10 ]            # greater than
[ "$N" -lt 10 ]            # less than
[ "$N" -ge 10 ]            # greater than or equal
[ "$N" -le 10 ]            # less than or equal
```

**File tests:**

```bash
[ -f "$FILE" ]             # file exists and is a regular file
[ -d "$DIR" ]              # directory exists
[ -e "$PATH" ]             # anything exists (file, dir, link, etc.)
[ -r "$FILE" ]             # file is readable
[ -w "$FILE" ]             # file is writable
[ -x "$FILE" ]             # file is executable
[ -s "$FILE" ]             # file exists and is not empty
```

---

## Loops

### for Loop

```bash
# Iterate over a list
for item in one two three; do
    echo "Item: $item"
done

# Iterate over files
for f in /etc/*.conf; do
    echo "Config file: $f"
done

# C-style for loop
for ((i=1; i<=10; i++)); do
    echo "Number: $i"
done
```

### while Loop — Reading Files Line by Line

```bash
# CORRECT way to read a file line by line
while IFS= read -r line; do
    echo "Line: $line"
done < /etc/passwd
```

Why this specific syntax?
- **IFS=** prevents leading/trailing whitespace from being stripped
- **-r** prevents backslash sequences from being interpreted
- **< file** redirects the file into the loop's stdin

**NEVER do this:**

```bash
# WRONG — breaks on spaces, expands globs, loads entire file into memory
for line in $(cat /etc/passwd); do
    echo "$line"
done
```

---

## Functions

```bash
# Logging function
log() {
    echo "[$(date +%H:%M:%S)] $*"
}

# Error function
die() {
    log "ERROR: $*"
    exit 1
}

# Usage
log "Starting deployment"
[ -f "$CONFIG" ] || die "Config file not found: $CONFIG"
log "Deployment complete"
```

Functions receive arguments as `$1`, `$2`, etc. `$*` and `$@` represent all arguments. `$#` is the count.

---

## Script Arguments

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# $0 = script name, $1 = first arg, $2 = second arg
# $@ = all arguments, $# = argument count

echo "Script: $0"
echo "First argument: ${1:-none}"
echo "All arguments: $@"
echo "Argument count: $#"

# Validate arguments
if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <filename>"
    exit 1
fi
```

---

## Error Handling

### Inline Error Handling

```bash
# If command fails, run the block
cd /opt/myapp || { echo "Cannot cd to /opt/myapp"; exit 1; }

# Same pattern for critical operations
mkdir -p "$BACKUP_DIR" || die "Cannot create backup directory"
```

### trap — Run Code on Exit or Error

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Clean up temp files on exit (normal or error)
TMP_FILE=$(mktemp)
trap 'rm -f "$TMP_FILE"' EXIT

# Log errors with line number
trap 'echo "Error at line $LINENO" >&2' ERR

# Your script logic here
echo "Working with $TMP_FILE"
```

`trap` is essential for cleanup. The `EXIT` trap runs when the script exits for any reason. The `ERR` trap runs when a command fails (with `set -e`).

---

## Debugging

```bash
# Syntax check (does not execute)
bash -n script.sh

# Trace mode (prints every command before executing)
bash -x script.sh

# Enable trace inside a script for a specific section
set -x
# ... commands to debug ...
set +x
```

`bash -x` is invaluable for debugging. It shows exactly what the shell is doing, with variables expanded.

---

## Complete Example: Log Rotation Script

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Configuration
LOG_DIR="/var/log/myapp"
MAX_AGE_DAYS=30
BACKUP_DIR="/backup/logs"

# Functions
log() { echo "[$(date +%H:%M:%S)] $*"; }
die() { log "ERROR: $*"; exit 1; }

# Cleanup on exit
trap 'log "Script finished"' EXIT
trap 'echo "Error at line $LINENO" >&2' ERR

# Validate
[ -d "$LOG_DIR" ] || die "Log directory not found: $LOG_DIR"
mkdir -p "$BACKUP_DIR"

# Compress logs older than 1 day
log "Compressing old logs..."
find "$LOG_DIR" -name "*.log" -mtime +1 -exec gzip {} \;

# Move compressed logs to backup
log "Moving to backup..."
find "$LOG_DIR" -name "*.gz" -exec mv {} "$BACKUP_DIR/" \;

# Delete backups older than MAX_AGE_DAYS
log "Cleaning old backups..."
find "$BACKUP_DIR" -name "*.gz" -mtime +"$MAX_AGE_DAYS" -delete

log "Done. Cleaned logs older than $MAX_AGE_DAYS days."
```

---

## Chapter Summary

- **Shebang:** `#!/bin/bash` — always include it.
- **Defensive settings:** `set -euo pipefail` — scripts fail loudly on errors, unset variables, and pipe failures.
- **Variables:** `NAME="value"`, `$(command)` for substitution, `${1:-default}` for defaults.
- **Quoting:** Double quotes expand variables; single quotes are literal. Always quote `"$VAR"`.
- **Conditionals:** `[ -f file ]`, `[ "$VAR" = "value" ]`, `[ "$N" -gt 10 ]`.
- **Loops:** `for item in list; do`, `while IFS= read -r line; do ... done < file`. Never `for line in $(cat file)`.
- **Functions:** `log() { echo "[$(date)] $*"; }` — reusable blocks.
- **Error handling:** `command || { echo "failed"; exit 1; }`, `trap 'cleanup' EXIT`.
- **Debugging:** `bash -x` traces execution, `bash -n` checks syntax.

### Q: What does `set -euo pipefail` do and why should every script use it?

`set -e` exits the script immediately if any command returns a non-zero exit code (fails). `set -u` treats references to unset variables as errors — this catches typos like `$TAGET_DIR` instead of `$TARGET_DIR`. `set -o pipefail` makes a pipeline return the exit code of the first failing command, not just the last one. Together, they make scripts **fail loudly** instead of silently continuing after errors, which prevents cascading damage like deleting the wrong directory.

### Q: Why should you never use `for line in $(cat file)` to read a file?

This pattern has three problems: (1) **Word splitting** — lines containing spaces are split into multiple iterations. (2) **Glob expansion** — characters like `*` are expanded to filenames. (3) **Memory** — the entire file is loaded into memory at once. The correct way is `while IFS= read -r line; do ... done < file`, which reads one line at a time, preserves whitespace (`IFS=`), and does not interpret backslashes (`-r`).

title: Command Reference & Cheat Sheet
description: A practical command reference organized by what you're trying to do, covering CPU, memory, disk, processes, files, users, network, services, logs, and package management.
content:
## CPU & Performance

"My server feels slow — what's eating the CPU?"

- `top` — Live dashboard. Shows CPU %, memory, and every process. Press `1` to see per-core. Press `M` to sort by memory. Press `P` to sort by CPU. Press `q` to quit. **This is your first stop when something feels wrong.**
- `htop` — Prettier version of top. Color-coded. Use arrow keys. Press F9 to kill a process. Install with `apt install htop` or `yum install htop`.
- `uptime` — One-liner showing load averages (1, 5, 15 min). For 4 cores, load of 4.0 = 100%. Above that = overloaded. If 1-min is way higher than 15-min, the problem is getting worse right now.
- `vmstat 1 5` — 5 snapshots, 1 second apart. This is the Swiss Army knife of performance diagnosis. Look at: `wa` (IO wait — high means disk is slow), `us`+`sy` (CPU usage), `si`/`so` (swap — non-zero means RAM is exhausted). If you learn one diagnostic command, make it this one.
- `mpstat -P ALL 1` — Per-CPU breakdown. Useful when one core is maxed but others are idle — that means a single-threaded process is hogging one core.
- `nproc` — How many CPU cores do I have? Quick answer to "what load average is too high for this machine?"
- `nice -n 10 command` — Run a command with lower priority (won't hog CPU from more important processes). Nice values range from -20 (highest priority) to 19 (lowest).
- `renice -n 5 -p PID` — Change priority of a running process. Need root for negative (higher priority) values.

---

## Memory

"Is the server running out of RAM?"

- `free -h` — Shows total, used, free, available. **Don't panic if 'free' is low** — Linux uses spare RAM as disk cache (that's good). Look at the `available` column — that's what apps can actually use. If available is healthy, your memory is fine regardless of what 'free' says.
- `cat /proc/meminfo` — Detailed memory breakdown. Shows MemTotal, MemFree, MemAvailable, Buffers, Cached, SwapTotal, SwapFree, and dozens more fields. Useful when you need specifics beyond what `free` shows.
- `vmstat 1` — Check `si` and `so` columns. If they're non-zero, the system is actively swapping (RAM exhausted, using slow disk instead). This is the single most important indicator of memory pressure.
- `ps aux --sort=-%mem | head` — Top memory consumers. Shows the processes eating the most RAM, sorted from highest to lowest. The `%MEM` and `RSS` columns tell you what each process is actually using.
- `swapon --show` — Is swap enabled? How much is used? If swap usage is high and growing, you have a memory problem.

---

## Disk & Storage

"Disk is full — what do I do?"

- `df -h` — Which filesystems are full? Shows mount point, size, used, available, use%. This is always your first command when you suspect disk issues. Look for any filesystem at or near 100%.
- `df -i` — Inode usage. Disk can show space available but still fail if inodes are exhausted (millions of tiny files). If writes fail but `df -h` looks fine, check this immediately.
- `du -sh /var/*` — What's using space in /var? Drill down into the biggest directory. Replace /var with whatever `df -h` showed as the full filesystem.
- `du -sh /* 2>/dev/null | sort -rh | head` — Top-level space hogs across the entire system. The `2>/dev/null` suppresses permission errors, `sort -rh` sorts largest first.
- `find /var/log -size +100M` — Find large log files. Runaway logs are the #1 cause of full disks on servers.
- `lsof | grep deleted` — Files deleted but still held open by a process (space won't free until process restarts). This is the classic reason `df` and `du` disagree.
- `> /var/log/bigfile.log` — Truncate a file in-place (frees space immediately, unlike rm which waits for process to close it). Use this instead of rm when a process has the file open.
- `ncdu /` — Interactive disk usage explorer. Navigate with arrow keys, drill into directories. Install with `apt install ncdu` or `yum install ncdu`. The best visual tool for hunting disk usage.

---

## Processes

"What's running? How do I stop it?"

- `ps aux` — Snapshot of all processes. Columns: USER, PID, %CPU, %MEM, COMMAND. This is a point-in-time view, not live.
- `ps aux | grep nginx` — Find a specific process. Add `grep -v grep` to exclude the grep process itself from results.
- `ps -ef --forest` — Process tree showing parent-child relationships. Great for understanding which process spawned which.
- `top` — Live view, sorted by CPU by default. Refreshes every few seconds so you can watch trends.
- `kill PID` — Send SIGTERM (polite: "please stop"). Always try this first. The process gets a chance to clean up.
- `kill -9 PID` — Send SIGKILL (forced: kernel kills it, no cleanup). Last resort. Can leave temp files, corrupt data, and leak resources.
- `killall nginx` — Kill all processes named nginx. Convenient but be careful — make sure you mean ALL of them.
- `pkill -u username` — Kill all processes owned by a user. Nuclear option for a specific user's processes.
- `jobs` — List background jobs in current shell session.
- `fg %1` — Bring job 1 to foreground.
- `bg %1` — Resume job 1 in background.
- `nohup command &` — Run command that survives SSH disconnect. Output goes to nohup.out by default.
- `pgrep -la nginx` — Find PIDs matching a name. Cleaner than `ps aux | grep`.

---

## Files & Directories

"How do I find, read, create, and manage files?"

- `ls -la` — List all files with permissions, owner, size, date. The `-a` shows hidden dotfiles.
- `ls -lh` — Same but human-readable sizes (KB, MB, GB) instead of raw bytes.
- `find /path -name '*.log'` — Find files by name. Searches recursively through all subdirectories.
- `find /path -mtime +7` — Files modified more than 7 days ago. Use `-mtime -1` for files modified in the last 24 hours.
- `find /path -size +100M` — Files larger than 100MB. Great for hunting space hogs.
- `find /path -type f -exec chmod 644 {} \;` — Find and act on files. The `{}` is replaced with each filename, `\;` ends the command.
- `locate filename` — Fast file search (uses pre-built index, run `updatedb` to refresh). Much faster than find for simple name searches.
- `cat file` — Print entire file to terminal. Good for small files only.
- `less file` — Paginated view. Press q to quit, / to search forward, n for next match, b to go back a page. Use this for anything longer than a screenful.
- `head -20 file` — First 20 lines. Quick peek at file format or headers.
- `tail -20 file` — Last 20 lines. See the most recent entries in a log.
- `tail -f /var/log/syslog` — Follow a log file in real-time (Ctrl+C to stop). Essential for watching logs while troubleshooting.
- `grep 'error' file` — Find lines containing 'error'. Add `-i` for case-insensitive.
- `grep -r 'pattern' /path/` — Search recursively in all files under a directory.
- `wc -l file` — Count lines in a file. Pipe other commands into this for counting.
- `stat file` — Detailed file info: inode number, permissions (octal and symbolic), timestamps, size, block count.
- `file myfile` — Detect file type by examining contents, not extension. Tells you if something is a binary, text, image, etc.

---

## Users & Permissions

"Who's on this system? Can they access this file?"

- `whoami` — Who am I? Prints your current username.
- `id` — My UID, GID, and all groups. More detailed than whoami.
- `who` / `w` — Who's logged in right now? `w` also shows what they're running and system load.
- `last` — Login history. Shows who logged in, from where, and when. Reads from /var/log/wtmp.
- `cat /etc/passwd` — All user accounts. Format: username:x:UID:GID:comment:home:shell.
- `cat /etc/group` — All groups and their members.
- `sudo -l` — What can I run as sudo? Shows your sudo privileges without actually running anything.
- `chmod 755 file` — Set permissions: owner rwx (7), group rx (5), others rx (5). Standard for scripts and directories.
- `chmod u+x file` — Add execute permission for owner only. Use symbolic notation for targeted changes.
- `chown user:group file` — Change owner and group. Needs sudo. Add `-R` for recursive.
- `passwd` — Change your own password. Use `sudo passwd username` to change someone else's.
- `useradd -m -s /bin/bash newuser` — Create user with home directory (-m) and bash shell (-s). Always use both flags for human users.
- `usermod -aG sudo username` — Add user to sudo group. **THE -a IS CRITICAL** — without it, you replace ALL their groups with just sudo, potentially locking them out of everything else.

---

## Network

"Can I reach that server? What's listening on what port?"

- `ip addr show` — My IP addresses and interfaces. Replaces the old `ifconfig`.
- `ip route show` — Routing table and default gateway. Shows where traffic goes.
- `ping -c 4 hostname` — Can I reach it? Sends 4 ICMP packets then stops. If this fails, it's a network or firewall issue.
- `traceroute hostname` — Show every hop between you and the destination. Helps identify where the path breaks.
- `dig domain.com` — DNS lookup. Shows the full DNS response including TTL. `dig @8.8.8.8 domain.com` bypasses local DNS to test with Google's resolver.
- `curl -I https://url` — HTTP response headers only (status code, server, content-type). Quick way to check if a web service is responding.
- `curl -v https://url` — Verbose HTTP request. Shows DNS resolution, TCP connection, TLS handshake, request headers, response headers, and body. The ultimate HTTP debugging tool.
- `ss -tulnp` — All listening ports with process names. Replaces netstat. `-t` TCP, `-u` UDP, `-l` listening, `-n` numeric, `-p` process.
- `nc -zv host port` — Test if a specific TCP port is open. Quick and clean — just tells you open or closed.
- `tcpdump -i eth0 port 80` — Capture packets on interface for port 80. The nuclear option for network debugging. Needs root.
- `hostname` — Machine's hostname. Simple but you'd be surprised how often you need it.
- `cat /etc/resolv.conf` — DNS servers configured on this machine.
- `cat /etc/hosts` — Local hostname overrides. Checked before DNS. If DNS seems wrong, check here first.
- `ssh user@host` — Connect to a remote server via SSH. Add `-v` for verbose output when troubleshooting.
- `scp file user@host:/path` — Copy a file to a remote server over SSH. Use `-r` for directories.

---

## Services & Systemd

"Is the service running? How do I restart it?"

- `systemctl status nginx` — Is it running? Shows active/inactive, PID, memory usage, and recent log lines. Your first command for any service question.
- `systemctl start nginx` — Start the service now. Not persistent across reboots.
- `systemctl stop nginx` — Stop the service now.
- `systemctl restart nginx` — Stop + start. Use when config changes require a full restart.
- `systemctl reload nginx` — Reload config without stopping the service (sends SIGHUP). Not all services support this, but it avoids downtime when they do.
- `systemctl enable nginx` — Start on boot. Creates a symlink in the systemd target directory.
- `systemctl enable --now nginx` — Start on boot AND start right now. This is almost always what you want.
- `systemctl disable nginx` — Don't start on boot. Does not stop the currently running service.
- `systemctl is-enabled nginx` — Will it start on boot? Returns "enabled" or "disabled".
- `systemctl --failed` — List all failed services. Quick health check for the whole system.
- `journalctl -u nginx -n 50` — Last 50 log lines for nginx. Better than hunting through log files.
- `journalctl -u nginx --since '1 hour ago'` — Recent logs with a time filter.
- `journalctl -p err` — All error-level messages across all services. Great for finding problems you didn't know about.

---

## Logs & Debugging

"Something broke — where do I look?"

- `journalctl -u servicename` — Service-specific logs. This is your best first stop for any service issue. Systemd captures stdout and stderr automatically.
- `journalctl -f` — Follow all system logs in real-time. Like `tail -f` but for the entire journal.
- `journalctl -b -1` — Logs from the previous boot. Essential after a crash or unexpected reboot.
- `journalctl -p err --since '1 hour ago'` — Recent errors only. Filters out the noise.
- `tail -f /var/log/syslog` — Follow the traditional system log. Some applications still write here instead of using journald.
- `tail -f /var/log/auth.log` — Watch authentication events in real-time. SSH logins, sudo usage, failed passwords.
- `dmesg -T` — Kernel messages with human-readable timestamps. Shows hardware errors, driver issues, OOM kills.
- `dmesg -T | grep -i 'error\|oom\|kill'` — Filter kernel messages for problems. This catches hardware failures and out-of-memory events.
- `grep 'Failed password' /var/log/auth.log | awk '{print $11}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn` — Find SSH brute force IPs. Shows which IP addresses have the most failed login attempts, sorted by count.
- `strace -p PID` — Trace system calls of a running process. Shows exactly what the process is doing at the kernel level. Attach to a stuck process to see where it's blocked.
- `lsof -p PID` — What files does this process have open? Includes regular files, sockets, pipes, and devices.
- `lsof -i :80` — What process is using port 80? Quick way to find port conflicts.

---

## Package Management

"How do I install software?"

- `apt update && apt install package` — Debian/Ubuntu. Always `apt update` first to refresh the package index.
- `yum install package` — RHEL/CentOS/Amazon Linux. On newer systems, use `dnf install package`.
- `apt search keyword` — Search for packages by name or description on Debian/Ubuntu.
- `dpkg -l | grep package` — Is it installed? Shows version and status on Debian-based systems.
- `rpm -qa | grep package` — Is it installed? Same thing for RHEL-based systems.
- `which command` — Where is this command's binary? Shows the full path.
- `command -v cmd` — Does this command exist? Returns the path if found, nothing if not. Useful in scripts.

---

## Quick Diagnosis Cheat Sheet

| Symptom | First Command | What to Look For |
|---------|--------------|------------------|
| Server slow | `top` | High CPU process, high load average |
| Out of memory | `free -h` | Low 'available', check `vmstat` for si/so |
| Disk full | `df -h` | 100% usage on a filesystem |
| Inode exhaustion | `df -i` | 100% IUse% |
| Can't reach server | `ping IP` | Timeout = network/firewall issue |
| DNS not resolving | `dig domain` | NXDOMAIN or no answer |
| Port not reachable | `ss -tulnp` | Service not listening |
| Service crashed | `systemctl status svc` | Active: failed, check logs |
| High IO wait | `vmstat 1` | wa column > 20% |
| Swapping | `vmstat 1` | si/so > 0 |
| Deleted file using space | `lsof \| grep deleted` | Process holding file open |
| Permission denied | `ls -la file` | Check owner, group, permission bits |
| Can't SSH | `ssh -v user@host` | Verbose output shows where it fails |

Remember: troubleshooting is about methodology, not memorisation. Start broad (uptime, top, vmstat), identify the category of problem (CPU, memory, disk, network), then drill down with specific tools. Always check the logs — `journalctl -u service` answers most questions.

### Q: What is the single most useful diagnostic command on Linux?

`vmstat 1 5` — five snapshots, one second apart. In one view it tells you: are you CPU-bound (high us+sy, low wa)? IO-bound (high wa, processes in b column)? Memory-bound (non-zero si/so)? It answers "what kind of problem is this?" which is the most important first question. From there you know which specific tools to use next.

### Q: How do you find what process is using a specific port?

Use `ss -tulnp | grep :PORT` or `lsof -i :PORT`. Both show the process name and PID. `ss` is the modern replacement for `netstat` and is available on all current Linux distributions. `lsof` gives more detail about the process but requires root for full output.

title: Troubleshooting Playbook
description: Real-world troubleshooting scenarios with step-by-step diagnosis and resolution procedures for slow servers, full disks, SSH failures, web errors, and more.
content:
## How to Use This Playbook

This chapter is a collection of real scenarios you will face in interviews and on the job. Each scenario follows a consistent pattern: **Symptom → Diagnosis Steps → Resolution**. Treat these like runbooks — step-by-step procedures you can follow under pressure when something is broken and people are waiting.

The scenarios are ordered from most common to least common. Master the first three and you will handle 80% of real-world incidents.

---

## Scenario 1: "The server is slow"

This is the #1 interview question and the #1 real-world complaint. Your methodology matters more than any single command.

**Step 1:** `uptime` — Check load averages. Compare the 1-minute, 5-minute, and 15-minute values. If 1-min is much higher than 15-min, the problem is getting worse right now. If 1-min is lower, it's resolving. Compare load to core count (`nproc`) — load equal to core count means 100% utilisation.

**Step 2:** `top` — Is it CPU, memory, or IO? Look at the top process. Press `P` to sort by CPU, `M` to sort by memory. Note the process name and PID of the biggest consumer.

**Step 3:** `vmstat 1 5` — The definitive diagnostic tool. Five snapshots, one second apart. Read the output like this:

- High `us`+`sy`, low `wa` → **CPU-bound**. A process is consuming all available CPU. Find it with `top`, consider killing it, optimising it, or adding CPU capacity.
- High `wa`, `b` > 0 → **IO-bound**. The disk is slow or saturated. Check with `iostat -x 1`. Find the IO hog with `iotop` (install if needed). Could be a runaway log writer, a database doing full table scans, or a failing disk.
- `si`/`so` > 0 → **Memory-bound**. The system is swapping — moving data between RAM and disk because RAM is exhausted. This makes everything slow. Find the memory hog with `ps aux --sort=-%mem | head`. Add RAM or kill the hog.

**Step 4:** `free -h` — Confirm the memory situation. Remember the golden rule: low 'free' is normal because Linux uses spare RAM as disk cache. Low 'available' is the actual problem. If available is near zero AND vmstat shows si/so activity, you have confirmed memory exhaustion.

**Step 5:** `iostat -x 1` — Disk utilisation. Look at the `%util` column. Near 100% means the disk is saturated — it physically cannot handle more IO. Also check `await` (average IO wait time in ms) — high values mean the disk is slow.

**Step 6:** `journalctl --since '1 hour ago'` — Check for recent errors or changes. Maybe someone deployed bad code, a config change went wrong, or a cron job is running amok.

**Step 7:** Form a hypothesis BEFORE making changes. "The server is slow because process X is consuming 95% CPU due to a runaway loop" is a hypothesis. "Let me restart everything" is not. One change at a time so you know what fixed it.

---

## Scenario 2: "Disk is full"

Disk full is the second most common server issue. Applications crash, logs stop writing, databases corrupt. Here is the systematic approach.

**Step 1:** `df -h` — Which filesystem is full? Look for any line showing 100% or near 100% in the Use% column. Note the mount point — that tells you where to look.

**Step 2:** `du -sh /* 2>/dev/null | sort -rh | head` — Find the biggest top-level directories. The `2>/dev/null` suppresses permission errors, `sort -rh` puts the largest first.

**Step 3:** Drill down into the biggest directory. If /var is the culprit: `du -sh /var/* | sort -rh | head`. Then: `du -sh /var/log/* | sort -rh | head`. Keep drilling until you find the specific files.

**Step 4:** `find /var/log -size +500M` — Find runaway log files directly. A single application writing unbounded logs is the most common cause of full disks.

**Step 5:** `lsof | grep deleted` — Check for deleted files still held open by a running process. This is the sneaky one. If someone already deleted a large file but the process that was writing to it is still running, the space is NOT freed. The file is gone from the directory listing but the disk blocks are still allocated because the process has an open file descriptor.

**Step 6:** Fix it. To free space from a log file that a process has open, **truncate it in-place** rather than deleting it:

```bash
> /var/log/bigfile.log
```

This empties the file but keeps the file descriptor valid. If you use `rm` instead, the space won't be freed until the process closes the file or is restarted.

**Step 7:** Restart the process that was writing to the file. This closes the old file descriptor and starts fresh.

**Step 8:** Set up logrotate to prevent recurrence. Add a config in `/etc/logrotate.d/` for the application. Logrotate automatically rotates, compresses, and deletes old logs on a schedule.

**Step 9:** Also check: `df -i` — Could be inode exhaustion. This happens when you have millions of tiny files (common with mail servers, package caches, or session files). The disk shows space available but you can't create new files because all inodes are used. The fix is to delete the tiny files or recreate the filesystem with more inodes.

---

## Scenario 3: "Can't SSH into the server"

Work through this systematically, from the network layer up to the application layer.

**Step 1:** `ping server_ip` — Can you reach the server at all?

- **No response** → Network or firewall issue. Check: Is the server running? Are security groups / NACLs allowing ICMP? Is there a routing problem? Can you reach other servers on the same network?
- **Response received** → Network is fine. Continue to the next step.

**Step 2:** `nc -zv server_ip 22` — Is port 22 open?

- **Connection refused** → The SSH daemon (sshd) is not running on the server. You need console access (cloud provider's web console, IPMI, or physical access) to fix it. Once on the console: `systemctl status sshd`, then `systemctl start sshd`.
- **Connection timed out** → A firewall is blocking port 22. Check security groups (AWS), firewall rules (`iptables -L` or `ufw status`), and NACLs.
- **Connection succeeded** → Port is open, SSH daemon is running. Continue.

**Step 3:** `ssh -v user@server` — Verbose mode shows exactly where the connection fails:

- "Connection timed out" → Firewall (same as above, but sometimes only SSH traffic is blocked while nc gets through).
- "Connection refused" → sshd not running.
- "Permission denied (publickey)" → Wrong SSH key. Check: Is the correct key in `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` on the server? Are key permissions correct? (`chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_rsa`, `chmod 700 ~/.ssh/`, `chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys`).
- "Permission denied (password)" → Wrong password, or password auth is disabled in `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`.
- "Host key verification failed" → The server's host key has changed. This could mean the server was rebuilt (harmless) or a man-in-the-middle attack (dangerous). If you know the server was rebuilt: `ssh-keygen -R server_ip` to remove the old key.

**Step 4:** On the server (if you have console access): `systemctl status sshd` to check the service, `tail -50 /var/log/auth.log` to see authentication attempts and errors.

---

## Scenario 4: "Website is returning errors"

**Step 1:** What error exactly? `curl -I https://site.com`

- **5xx** (500, 502, 503, 504) → Server-side problem. The web server or its backend is broken.
- **4xx** (400, 401, 403, 404) → Client-side issue. Wrong URL, missing authentication, or forbidden resource.
- **Connection refused** → The web server process is not running.
- **Connection timed out** → Network or firewall issue.

**Step 2:** Is the web server running? `systemctl status nginx` (or `apache2` or `httpd`). If it's not running, start it and check why it stopped.

**Step 3:** Check web server error logs: `tail -50 /var/log/nginx/error.log`. The error log almost always tells you exactly what's wrong — missing files, permission errors, upstream timeouts, config syntax errors.

**Step 4:** Validate web server config: `nginx -t` (for nginx) or `apachectl configtest` (for Apache). A syntax error in the config will prevent the server from starting or reloading.

**Step 5:** Can the web server reach its backend? If you're running a reverse proxy setup: `curl -v http://localhost:8080/health`. If the backend is down, the web server returns 502 (Bad Gateway) or 504 (Gateway Timeout).

**Step 6:** Check disk space: `df -h`. Web servers can fail in strange ways when the disk is full — they can't write temp files, can't log, and some crash entirely.

**Step 7:** Check permissions: `ls -la /var/www/html/`. Can the web server user (usually `www-data` or `nginx`) read the files it's trying to serve? A recent deployment might have changed file ownership.

---

## Scenario 5: "A process is using too much CPU"

**Step 1:** `top` — Identify the process. Note the PID, command name, and %CPU. Is it a known service or something unexpected?

**Step 2:** `ps aux | grep PID` — Get the full command line. `top` truncates long commands, but `ps` shows the complete invocation including arguments.

**Step 3:** `strace -p PID -c` — What system calls is it making? The `-c` flag gives a summary: counts and time spent in each system call. If it's spending all its time in `read()` or `write()`, it might be stuck in a loop reading/writing files. If it's all `futex()`, it might be in a threading deadlock.

**Step 4:** `lsof -p PID` — What files does it have open? This shows regular files, network sockets, pipes, and devices. A process with thousands of open files might have a file descriptor leak.

**Step 5:** `ls -la /proc/PID/fd` — File descriptors. Another way to see what the process is connected to.

**Step 6:** Decision time. Kill it? `kill PID` (SIGTERM first — always). If it doesn't stop after a few seconds: `kill -9 PID`. If it's a managed service: `systemctl restart service` is cleaner because systemd handles the lifecycle properly.

**Step 7:** If it's a known service, check its logs: `journalctl -u service -n 100`. The logs often explain why the process went haywire — maybe it received bad input, hit an infinite loop, or a dependency failed.

---

## Scenario 6: "Users can't resolve DNS"

**Step 1:** `dig google.com` — Does DNS work from the server itself? If yes, the problem might be client-side. If no, continue.

**Step 2:** `dig @8.8.8.8 google.com` — Does it work when you bypass local DNS and use Google's public resolver? If yes → your local DNS server or configuration is broken. If no → you have a network connectivity problem (can't reach external DNS servers).

**Step 3:** `cat /etc/resolv.conf` — What nameservers are configured? Are they correct? Are they reachable? Sometimes this file gets overwritten by DHCP or cloud-init with wrong values.

**Step 4:** `ping nameserver_ip` — Can you reach the configured nameserver? If not, it might be down or unreachable due to network/firewall issues.

**Step 5:** `systemctl status systemd-resolved` — Is the local DNS resolver service running? On systems using systemd-resolved, this service handles DNS caching and forwarding. If it's crashed, nothing resolves.

**Step 6:** Check `/etc/hosts` — Any conflicting local overrides? Entries in /etc/hosts take precedence over DNS. A wrong entry here can make it look like DNS is broken when it's actually a local override.

---

## Scenario 7: "Service won't start on boot"

**Step 1:** `systemctl is-enabled service` — Is it even enabled for boot? If not: `systemctl enable service`. This is the most common cause — someone started the service manually but forgot to enable it.

**Step 2:** `systemctl start service` — Try starting it manually right now. Does it work?

**Step 3:** `systemctl status service` — Read the error message. Systemd usually gives you a clear indication: "failed to bind to port" (port conflict), "permission denied" (wrong user), "file not found" (missing binary or config).

**Step 4:** `journalctl -u service -n 50` — Full recent logs. The status output only shows a few lines; the journal has the complete picture.

**Step 5:** Common causes and fixes:
- **Config error** → Fix the config file, run `nginx -t` or equivalent to validate, then `systemctl start service`.
- **Port conflict** → Another service is already using the port. Find it with `ss -tulnp | grep :PORT`. Stop the conflicting service or change the port.
- **Missing dependency** → The service depends on another service that hasn't started yet. Check `After=` and `Requires=` in the unit file.
- **Wrong user/permissions** → The service is configured to run as a user that doesn't exist or can't access its files. Check the `User=` directive in the unit file and file permissions.
- **Disk full** → The service can't write its PID file, log file, or data. Check `df -h`.

---

## Scenario 8: "Permission denied when accessing a file"

**Step 1:** `ls -la file` — Check the permission bits, owner, and group. The output looks like `-rw-r----- 1 root www-data 4096 Mar 15 10:30 file`. Read it as: owner (root) has rw, group (www-data) has r, others have nothing.

**Step 2:** `id` — What user am I, and what groups am I in? Compare this to the file's owner and group.

**Step 3:** Understand how Linux checks permissions, in order:
1. Are you the **owner**? → Apply owner bits (first rwx triplet).
2. Are you in the **group**? → Apply group bits (second rwx triplet).
3. **Neither**? → Apply others bits (third rwx triplet).

Linux stops at the first match. If you're the owner but owner bits deny access, it does NOT fall through to check group bits — even if you're in the group.

**Step 4:** `stat file` — Full details including the octal permission value and inode number.

**Step 5:** Check parent directories: `ls -la /path/to/`. You need execute (x) permission on **every directory in the path** to reach the file. A common mistake is setting correct permissions on the file but forgetting that a parent directory blocks access.

**Step 6:** Fix it:
- `chmod 644 file` — Give owner read/write, everyone else read.
- `chown user:group file` — Change ownership.
- `usermod -aG group user` — Add yourself to the file's group (remember the `-a` flag!). You'll need to log out and back in for group changes to take effect.

---

## Scenario 9: "System crashed / rebooted unexpectedly"

**Step 1:** `last reboot` — When did it reboot? Shows the timestamp of every reboot. Compare this to when the problem was reported.

**Step 2:** `journalctl -b -1` — Logs from the previous boot. This is the most valuable source of information. Scroll to the end to see what happened right before the crash. Look for error messages, OOM kills, or kernel panics.

**Step 3:** `dmesg -T | grep -i 'oom\|kill\|panic\|error'` — Kernel-level problems. The kernel ring buffer may contain messages about:
- **OOM kills**: "Out of memory: Kill process 1234 (java) score 950" — the system ran out of RAM and the kernel killed a process to survive.
- **Kernel panic**: A fatal kernel error, often caused by hardware failure or a kernel bug.
- **Hardware errors**: Disk failures, memory (ECC) errors, CPU exceptions.

**Step 4:** `cat /var/log/kern.log` — Kernel log file. May contain additional details not in dmesg.

**Step 5:** Common causes:
- **OOM Killer** — The system ran out of RAM and swap. The kernel killed the biggest memory consumer. Check dmesg for "Out of memory: Kill process". Fix: add RAM, fix the memory leak, or tune the OOM score to protect critical processes.
- **Kernel panic** — A fatal error in the kernel. Often caused by hardware failure (bad RAM, failing disk) or a kernel bug. Check dmesg for "Kernel panic" messages.
- **Hardware failure** — Disk errors, memory errors, overheating. Check dmesg for hardware-related messages. Check SMART data for disks: `smartctl -a /dev/sda`.
- **Someone ran `reboot`** — Check `last reboot` and `/var/log/auth.log` to see if someone intentionally rebooted the system.

---

## Scenario 10: "Network connectivity issues between two servers"

**Step 1:** Can server A ping server B's IP? `ping B_IP`

- **No response** → Routing or firewall issue. Check `ip route` on both servers. In cloud environments, check security groups and NACLs. Check if the servers are in the same VPC/subnet or if there's a peering/routing issue.
- **Response received** → Basic network connectivity is fine. Continue.

**Step 2:** Can server A reach server B's specific port? `nc -zv B_IP port`

- **Connection refused** → The service is not running on server B, or it's not listening on that port.
- **Connection timed out** → A firewall is blocking that specific port.
- **Connection succeeded** → Network and port are fine. The problem is at the application layer.

**Step 3:** On server B: `ss -tulnp | grep port` — Is the service actually listening on the expected port? Maybe it's listening on localhost (127.0.0.1) only instead of all interfaces (0.0.0.0). Check the service's bind address configuration.

**Step 4:** On server B: `iptables -L` or `ufw status` — Check OS-level firewall rules. Even if cloud security groups allow the traffic, the OS firewall might block it.

**Step 5:** In AWS (or other cloud): Check **security groups** (instance-level, stateful) AND **NACLs** (subnet-level, stateless). Remember that NACLs need rules for both inbound AND outbound traffic (including ephemeral ports for responses). A common mistake is allowing inbound on the NACL but forgetting the outbound rule for response traffic.

**Step 6:** `traceroute B_IP` — Where does the path break? Each hop is shown with its latency. If the trace stops at a certain point, that's where the routing or firewall is blocking.

---

## The Golden Rules of Troubleshooting

These rules apply to every scenario above and every incident you will ever face:

- **Gather data before making changes.** Don't restart things randomly hoping the problem goes away. Understand what's wrong first. Restarting might fix the symptom temporarily but masks the root cause, and it destroys the evidence you need to prevent recurrence.

- **Work the stack.** Physical → Network → OS → Application. Start at the bottom and work up. If the network is down, there's no point debugging the application. If the disk is full, the application error messages are a symptom, not the cause.

- **Check the logs.** `journalctl -u service` is almost always your answer. Logs exist to tell you what went wrong. Read them before guessing. The error message is usually right there, waiting for someone to look.

- **One change at a time.** If you change three things at once and the problem goes away, you don't know which change fixed it. You also don't know if the other two changes introduced new problems. Change one thing, test, then move on.

- **Document what you find.** Write down what you checked, what you found, and what you changed. Future you (or the next on-call engineer) will thank you. A good incident report prevents the same problem from happening again.

- **Ask: what changed?** Most problems are caused by recent changes. A deployment, a config change, a security patch, a scaling event, a certificate expiration. If the system was working yesterday and isn't today, something changed. Find out what.

- **Don't panic.** Methodical troubleshooting beats frantic guessing every time. Take a breath, follow the steps, and trust the process. The answer is in the data.

### Q: What is your troubleshooting methodology when someone reports "the server is slow"?

I follow a systematic approach: (1) `uptime` to check load averages and trend. (2) `top` to identify the top resource consumer. (3) `vmstat 1 5` to classify the bottleneck — high us+sy means CPU-bound, high wa means IO-bound, non-zero si/so means memory-bound (swapping). (4) `free -h` to confirm memory status (check 'available', not 'free'). (5) `iostat -x 1` for disk utilisation. (6) `df -h` for disk space. (7) `journalctl` and `dmesg` for errors. The vmstat output is the key — it tells me which category of problem I'm dealing with, and from there I use targeted tools to find the specific cause.

### Q: A user reports "disk full" but df -h shows space available. What do you check?

Two things: First, `df -i` to check inode usage. If IUse% is at 100%, the filesystem has run out of inodes even though there's space available. This happens with millions of tiny files. Second, check if the user is on a different filesystem than what `df -h` shows as having space. The full filesystem might be a specific mount point like /home or /var that's separate from the root filesystem. Also verify the user isn't hitting a disk quota with `quota -u username`.

### Q: How do you handle a situation where you can't SSH into a production server?

I work through it layer by layer: (1) `ping` the IP to verify basic network connectivity. (2) `nc -zv IP 22` to test if port 22 is open — connection refused means sshd is down, timeout means firewall. (3) If I can't reach it at all, I check security groups, NACLs, and routing in the cloud console. (4) I use the cloud provider's console access (AWS Session Manager, EC2 Serial Console, or web-based console) to get onto the server without SSH. (5) Once on the server, I check `systemctl status sshd`, review `/var/log/auth.log`, and verify the SSH config and key permissions.

title: Networking Fundamentals
description: Understand TCP/IP, DNS, the OSI model, common protocols and ports, and essential networking commands for troubleshooting.
content:
## What Happens When You Type www.amazon.com?

This is the most common networking interview question. Here is the complete journey:

1. **Browser cache** — the browser checks if it already has the IP address for www.amazon.com cached.
2. **OS cache** — the operating system checks its own DNS cache.
3. **Router cache** — your home/office router may have the answer cached.
4. **ISP DNS cache** — your Internet Service Provider's DNS resolver checks its cache.
5. **DNS resolution** — if nobody has it cached, the ISP's resolver performs a full lookup:
   - Asks a **root nameserver** ("Who handles .com?")
   - Asks the **.com nameserver** ("Who handles amazon.com?")
   - Asks **amazon.com's nameserver** ("What is the IP for www.amazon.com?")
   - Gets back an IP address (e.g., 205.251.242.103)
6. **TCP 3-way handshake** — your browser establishes a TCP connection to that IP on port 443 (HTTPS).
7. **TLS handshake** — encryption is negotiated (certificates, keys).
8. **HTTP GET request** — the browser sends `GET / HTTP/1.1` with headers.
9. **Server response** — Amazon's server sends back HTML, CSS, JavaScript.
10. **Rendering** — the browser parses HTML, fetches additional resources, and renders the page.

---

## TCP vs UDP

These are the two main transport-layer protocols. Understanding their differences is fundamental.

### TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)

- **Connection-oriented** — establishes a connection before sending data (3-way handshake)
- **Reliable** — guarantees delivery. If a packet is lost, TCP retransmits it.
- **Ordered** — packets arrive in the correct sequence
- **Flow control** — adjusts speed to prevent overwhelming the receiver
- **Higher overhead** — all these guarantees cost extra headers and round trips

**Used by:** HTTP/HTTPS, SSH, FTP, SMTP (email), databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL)

### UDP (User Datagram Protocol)

- **Connectionless** — no handshake, just sends packets
- **Unreliable** — no guarantee of delivery. Lost packets are not retransmitted.
- **Unordered** — packets may arrive out of order
- **Lower overhead** — minimal headers, no connection state
- **Faster** — no round trips for setup

**Used by:** DNS (small queries, speed matters), DHCP, streaming video/audio, online gaming, VoIP

**Why does DNS use UDP?** DNS queries are small (fit in one packet), need to be fast, and if a response is lost, the client simply asks again. The overhead of a TCP handshake for each tiny query would be wasteful. (Note: DNS falls back to TCP for large responses like zone transfers.)

---

## The 3-Way Handshake

TCP establishes a connection with three packets:

```
Client                    Server
  |                         |
  |--- SYN (seq=100) ------>|   1. Client: "I want to connect. My sequence starts at 100."
  |                         |
  |<-- SYN/ACK (seq=300, ---|   2. Server: "OK. My sequence starts at 300. I acknowledge your 100."
  |    ack=101)             |
  |                         |
  |--- ACK (ack=301) ------>|   3. Client: "I acknowledge your 300. Connection established."
  |                         |
```

After this, data flows in both directions. The connection is closed with a 4-way handshake (FIN/ACK from each side).

---

## The OSI Model

The **Open Systems Interconnection** model describes networking in 7 layers. Each layer has a specific job and communicates with the layers above and below it.

| Layer | Name | Function | Protocols | Data Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | **Application** | User-facing services | HTTP, HTTPS, SSH, FTP, DNS, SMTP | Data |
| 6 | **Presentation** | Encryption, compression, encoding | TLS/SSL, JPEG, ASCII | Data |
| 5 | **Session** | Manages sessions/connections | NetBIOS, RPC | Data |
| 4 | **Transport** | End-to-end delivery, reliability | TCP, UDP | Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP) |
| 3 | **Network** | Routing between networks | IP, ICMP, ARP | Packet |
| 2 | **Data Link** | Node-to-node delivery on same network | Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11) | Frame |
| 1 | **Physical** | Raw bits on the wire | Cables, radio waves, fiber | Bits |

**Memory aid:** "All People Seem To Need Data Processing" (top to bottom) or "Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away" (bottom to top).

In practice, most troubleshooting happens at layers 3 (IP/routing), 4 (TCP/UDP ports), and 7 (application protocols).

---

## IP Addresses

- **IPv4:** 32-bit address, written as four octets: `192.168.1.100`. About 4.3 billion addresses (running out).
- **IPv6:** 128-bit address, written in hex: `2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334`. Virtually unlimited.
- **Subnet mask / Network mask:** Defines which part of the IP is the network and which is the host. Example: `255.255.255.0` or `/24` means the first 24 bits are the network.

---

## Key Protocols

### ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol)

ICMP is used for **error reporting** and **diagnostics**. It is NOT TCP or UDP — it is its own protocol at the network layer.

- **ping** uses ICMP Echo Request/Reply to test if a host is reachable
- **traceroute** uses ICMP (or UDP) to map the path packets take
- ICMP is connectionless — no handshake
- Firewalls often block ICMP, so "ping fails" does not always mean "host is down"

### DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol)

DHCP automatically assigns IP addresses to devices on a network. When your laptop connects to Wi-Fi:

1. **DHCP Discover** — your device broadcasts "I need an IP address"
2. **DHCP Offer** — the DHCP server offers an available IP
3. **DHCP Request** — your device accepts the offer
4. **DHCP Acknowledge** — the server confirms; your device now has an IP, subnet mask, gateway, and DNS server

DHCP uses UDP (ports 67/68).

### HTTP and HTTPS

- **HTTP** (port 80) — unencrypted web traffic
- **HTTPS** (port 443) — HTTP encrypted with TLS/SSL. The server presents a certificate to prove its identity, then all traffic is encrypted.

### DNS Record Types

| Record | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Maps domain to IPv4 address | `amazon.com → 205.251.242.103` |
| **AAAA** | Maps domain to IPv6 address | `amazon.com → 2600:9000:...` |
| **CNAME** | Alias — maps one domain to another domain | `www.amazon.com → amazon.com` |
| **NS** | Nameserver — which DNS server is authoritative | `amazon.com NS ns1.amazon.com` |
| **MX** | Mail server for the domain | `amazon.com MX mail.amazon.com` |

### Iterative vs Recursive DNS

- **Recursive:** Your client asks one resolver, and that resolver does all the work (asks root, .com, amazon.com) and returns the final answer. This is what your ISP's DNS does.
- **Iterative:** The resolver asks root, gets a referral to .com, asks .com, gets a referral to amazon.com, asks amazon.com, gets the answer. Each step returns a referral, not the final answer.

---

## Common Ports

| Port | Protocol | Service |
|---|---|---|
| 22 | TCP | SSH |
| 80 | TCP | HTTP |
| 443 | TCP | HTTPS |
| 53 | UDP/TCP | DNS |
| 20/21 | TCP | FTP (data/control) |
| 25 | TCP | SMTP (email sending) |
| 3306 | TCP | MySQL |
| 5432 | TCP | PostgreSQL |
| 6379 | TCP | Redis |
| 3389 | TCP | RDP (Remote Desktop) |

---

## Networking Commands

### ip addr show — View Network Interfaces

```bash
ip addr show
# Shows all interfaces with IP addresses, MAC addresses, and state

ip addr show eth0              # specific interface
```

### ip route — View Routing Table

```bash
ip route
# default via 10.0.0.1 dev eth0    ← default gateway
# 10.0.0.0/24 dev eth0              ← local network
```

### ss — Socket Statistics (Replaces netstat)

```bash
ss -tulnp
# -t TCP, -u UDP, -l listening, -n numeric (no DNS), -p process name

# Find what is listening on port 80
ss -tlnp | grep :80
```

### ping — Test Connectivity

```bash
ping 8.8.8.8                   # ping Google's DNS (tests basic connectivity)
ping -c 4 amazon.com           # send 4 pings then stop
```

### traceroute — Trace the Path

```bash
traceroute amazon.com          # shows each hop between you and the destination
```

### dig — DNS Lookup

```bash
dig amazon.com                 # query DNS for A record
dig amazon.com MX              # query for mail servers
dig @8.8.8.8 amazon.com        # query using Google's DNS server
```

### curl — HTTP Requests

```bash
curl https://amazon.com        # fetch a web page
curl -I https://amazon.com     # headers only
curl -v https://amazon.com     # verbose (shows TLS handshake, headers)
```

### nc (netcat) — Test Port Connectivity

```bash
nc -zv amazon.com 443          # test if port 443 is open
nc -zv 10.0.0.5 22             # test SSH port
```

### tcpdump — Packet Capture

```bash
sudo tcpdump -i eth0 port 80   # capture HTTP traffic
sudo tcpdump -i eth0 host 10.0.0.5   # capture traffic to/from a host
```

---

## Key Configuration Files

```bash
# Static hostname-to-IP mappings (checked before DNS)
cat /etc/hosts
# 127.0.0.1   localhost
# 10.0.0.5    myserver

# DNS resolver configuration
cat /etc/resolv.conf
# nameserver 10.0.0.2
# nameserver 8.8.8.8
```

---

## SSH Troubleshooting Methodology

When `ssh user@hostname` fails:

1. **"Could not resolve hostname"** → DNS problem. Try `ssh user@IP_ADDRESS` instead. Check `/etc/resolv.conf` and `dig hostname`.
2. **"Connection timed out"** → Firewall is blocking port 22. Check security groups, iptables, or network ACLs.
3. **"Connection refused"** → The SSH service is not running on the target. Check `systemctl status sshd` on the server.
4. **"Permission denied"** → Authentication failed. Check username, SSH key, key permissions (`chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_rsa`).

### General Network Diagnostic Methodology

```bash
# Can I reach the IP?
ping 10.0.0.5
# YES → network is fine, check application/port
# NO → routing or firewall issue

# Can I reach by hostname?
ping myserver.example.com
# YES → DNS is fine
# NO but ping IP works → DNS problem

# Is the port open?
nc -zv 10.0.0.5 22
# YES → service is running
# NO → service is down or firewall blocks the port
```

---

## Chapter Summary

- **TCP** is reliable, ordered, connection-oriented (HTTP, SSH). **UDP** is fast, connectionless, no guarantees (DNS, streaming).
- **3-way handshake:** SYN → SYN/ACK → ACK.
- **OSI model:** 7 layers from Physical to Application.
- **DNS** resolves domain names to IP addresses. Uses UDP for speed. Record types: A, AAAA, CNAME, NS, MX.
- **ICMP** is for diagnostics (ping, traceroute). **DHCP** assigns IP addresses automatically.
- **Common ports:** SSH=22, HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, DNS=53.
- **Key commands:** `ip addr`, `ss -tulnp`, `ping`, `dig`, `curl`, `nc -zv`, `traceroute`.
- **SSH troubleshooting:** resolve error → DNS; timeout → firewall; refused → service down; denied → auth.

### Q: What happens when you type www.amazon.com in a browser?

The browser checks its DNS cache, then the OS cache, router cache, and ISP cache. If no cache hit, the ISP's DNS resolver performs iterative lookups: root nameserver → .com nameserver → amazon.com nameserver → returns the IP address. The browser then performs a TCP 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN/ACK, ACK) to the server's IP on port 443, negotiates TLS encryption, sends an HTTP GET request, receives the HTML response, and renders the page. Additional resources (CSS, JS, images) trigger further requests.

### Q: What is the difference between TCP and UDP?

**TCP** is connection-oriented (3-way handshake), reliable (retransmits lost packets), and ordered. It is used for HTTP, SSH, databases — anything where data integrity matters. **UDP** is connectionless, unreliable (no retransmission), and unordered, but has lower overhead and is faster. It is used for DNS, DHCP, streaming, and gaming — where speed matters more than guaranteed delivery.

### Q: How does DNS work?

DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names to IP addresses. When a query is not cached, a **recursive resolver** (usually your ISP's) performs iterative lookups: it asks a root nameserver which directs it to the TLD nameserver (.com), which directs it to the authoritative nameserver for the domain, which returns the IP address. Results are cached at each level with a TTL (Time To Live). DNS primarily uses UDP port 53 for speed, falling back to TCP for large responses.

title: Advanced Networking & VPCs
description: Understand routing vs switching, network devices, VPCs, subnets, security groups, NACLs, NAT gateways, CIDR notation, VPN, and proxy servers.
content:
## Network Devices: Routing vs Switching

Before diving into cloud networking, you need to understand the physical devices that move data around.

### Hub (Layer 1 — Physical)

A hub is the simplest device. When it receives data on one port, it **broadcasts it to ALL other ports**. Every device on the hub sees every packet. This is wasteful and insecure. Hubs are obsolete.

### Bridge (Layer 2 — Data Link)

A bridge connects two network segments. It **buffers frames** and forwards them based on MAC addresses. Smarter than a hub but limited to two segments.

### Switch (Layer 2 — Data Link)

A switch is a multi-port bridge. It maintains a **MAC address forwarding table** that maps each MAC address to a specific port. When a frame arrives, the switch looks up the destination MAC and sends it **only to the correct port**. This is far more efficient than a hub.

- Operates at **Layer 2** (Data Link)
